6-study-skills-executive-functioning-pla.prompt: What should you mark first when building a weekly study plan?

options.text: Fixed commitments like practice or family events
options.feedback: Correct. The routine starts by mapping the times you already have to use.

options.text: Every subject you might want to study
options.feedback: You choose study blocks after you see the real open time in your week.

options.text: The longest possible homework session
options.feedback: Long sessions are not the first step and are often less realistic.

options.text: A reward for finishing all your work
options.feedback: Rewards can help, but they are not the first move in the routine.

6-study-skills-executive-functioning-pla.explanation: Map your fixed commitments first so you can plan around the week you actually have.

6-study-skills-executive-functioning-pla.prompt: What is an anchor block in this lesson?

options.text: The main study block you protect first for the hardest or most important subject
options.feedback: Correct. The anchor block is the first study block you place on purpose.

options.text: A free period where you can do anything you want
options.feedback: An anchor block has a clear academic job.

options.text: A long break after school every day
options.feedback: The routine does not require a long daily break.

options.text: A class assignment that is already finished
options.feedback: An anchor block is scheduled time, not finished work.

6-study-skills-executive-functioning-pla.explanation: The anchor block is the main focused study time you place first.

6-study-skills-executive-functioning-pla.prompt: Why are review blocks kept short in the routine?

options.text: Short review blocks are easier to fit into a real week and easier to keep
options.feedback: Correct. Short, focused review blocks are realistic and repeatable.

options.text: Short blocks mean the work does not need attention
options.feedback: Review work still matters, even if the block is short.

options.text: Students should never spend more than ten minutes studying
options.feedback: The lesson recommends short review blocks, not a limit on all studying.

options.text: Review blocks only happen when all homework is finished
options.feedback: Review blocks can be planned even when other work is still coming.

6-study-skills-executive-functioning-pla.explanation: Short review blocks work because they are realistic and focused.

6-study-skills-executive-functioning-pla.prompt: Which task fits best in a review block?

options.text: Rereading notes and quizzing yourself on vocabulary
options.feedback: Correct. A review block is meant for short, focused follow-up work.

options.text: Starting a brand-new month-long project
options.feedback: That task is too large for a short review block.

options.text: Waiting to see if homework appears later
options.feedback: A review block should have a clear study job.

options.text: Packing every unfinished subject into one sitting
options.feedback: That overloads the block instead of keeping it focused.

6-study-skills-executive-functioning-pla.explanation: Review blocks work best for rereading, self-quizzing, and checking recent work.

6-study-skills-executive-functioning-pla.prompt: A student has robotics on Monday, band on Wednesday, and a family dinner out on Friday. Which plan uses the routine best?

options.text: Tuesday 25-minute math anchor block, Thursday 15-minute reading review, Saturday 15-minute vocabulary review
options.feedback: Correct. The plan protects one anchor block and spreads two short review blocks across open times.

options.text: Monday 60 minutes of math right after robotics, plus nothing else all week
options.feedback: That depends on one heavy block at a poor time.

options.text: Wednesday 30 minutes of reading during band and Friday 30 minutes before dinner
options.feedback: Those times conflict with fixed commitments.

options.text: Put all study tasks on Friday night after the dinner out
options.feedback: That plan is overloaded and not realistic.

6-study-skills-executive-functioning-pla.explanation: A strong plan respects fixed commitments, protects one anchor block, and spreads review.

6-study-skills-executive-functioning-pla.prompt: What should you do if all three study blocks are packed onto one busy evening?

options.text: Keep one anchor block and move the review blocks to other open times
options.feedback: Correct. The routine works better when the week is balanced.

options.text: Make the anchor block even longer so everything gets done at once
options.feedback: A longer overloaded night usually makes the plan harder to keep.

options.text: Delete the anchor block and only leave easy tasks
options.feedback: The anchor block protects the most important work.

options.text: Leave the rest of the week empty because the plan is already written
options.feedback: The checking step is there so you can adjust the plan.

6-study-skills-executive-functioning-pla.explanation: Balanced plans are easier to follow than one overloaded night.

6-study-skills-executive-functioning-pla.prompt: What is the main reason to map fixed commitments before adding study blocks?

options.text: It prevents you from planning study time on top of things you already have to do
options.feedback: Correct. The routine begins with reality, not guessing.

options.text: It guarantees every day will have the same amount of homework
options.feedback: The routine cannot control how much homework appears.

options.text: It removes the need to choose a priority subject
options.feedback: You still need to decide what deserves the anchor block.

options.text: It means review blocks are no longer necessary
options.feedback: Review blocks are still part of the routine.

6-study-skills-executive-functioning-pla.explanation: Mapping fixed commitments first keeps the plan realistic and conflict-free.

6-study-skills-executive-functioning-pla.prompt: Which choice is the strongest anchor block?

options.text: A 25-minute math block on Tuesday right after snack, before anything else starts
options.feedback: Correct. It is focused, protected, and placed in a real open time.

options.text: A two-hour block late Sunday night when you are already tired
options.feedback: That is too long and poorly placed.

options.text: A block with no subject chosen yet
options.feedback: An anchor block should protect a real priority subject.

options.text: A block that starts during soccer practice
options.feedback: That conflicts with a fixed commitment.

6-study-skills-executive-functioning-pla.explanation: A strong anchor block is realistic, protected, and tied to a priority subject.

6-study-skills-executive-functioning-pla.prompt: What should you check before finalizing the weekly plan?

options.text: Whether the blocks conflict with commitments, overload one day, or need missing materials
options.feedback: Correct. The checking step catches the biggest planning problems.

options.text: Whether every block is exactly the same length
options.feedback: Blocks can be different lengths if the plan is still realistic.

options.text: Whether your friend uses the same schedule
options.feedback: The routine should fit your week, not someone else's.

options.text: Whether you can finish the whole month tonight
options.feedback: The routine is for one realistic week at a time.

6-study-skills-executive-functioning-pla.explanation: Checking the plan helps you catch conflicts, overload, and missing materials.

6-study-skills-executive-functioning-pla.prompt: A student misses Thursday's review block because of an unexpected appointment. What is the best response?

options.text: Move the short review block to the next realistic open time
options.feedback: Correct. The routine should be adjusted, not abandoned.

options.text: Cancel the whole weekly plan immediately
options.feedback: Missing one block does not ruin the rest of the plan.

options.text: Add the missed review block to an already overloaded night
options.feedback: That creates a new problem instead of fixing the first one.

options.text: Turn the missed review into a two-hour weekend session by default
options.feedback: The routine favors short, realistic moves, not giant makeup sessions.

6-study-skills-executive-functioning-pla.explanation: If one block is missed, move it to another realistic slot instead of giving up.

6-study-skills-executive-functioning-pla.prompt: Why does an anchor block help a student keep studying during the week?

options.text: It protects the hardest or most important subject by giving it a real time before the week gets crowded
options.feedback: Correct. The anchor block protects important work before other tasks take over the week.

options.text: It removes the need for any review blocks later in the week
options.feedback: The routine still includes review blocks.

options.text: It guarantees every assignment will take the same amount of time
options.feedback: An anchor block helps with planning, but it does not make all tasks equal.

options.text: It works only if the block is at least two hours long
options.feedback: The lesson recommends a realistic block of about 20 to 30 minutes.

6-study-skills-executive-functioning-pla.explanation: An anchor block helps because it protects the most important work at a real time in the week.

6-study-skills-executive-functioning-pla.prompt: Which pair should a student check before saying a weekly study plan is ready?

options.text: Whether any block conflicts with a commitment and whether one day is overloaded
options.feedback: Correct. Those are two of the main checks in the routine.

options.text: Whether every block uses the same subject and the same length
options.feedback: The routine does not require identical subjects or block lengths.

options.text: Whether a friend chose the same schedule and the same rewards
options.feedback: The plan should fit the student's actual week, not match someone else's.

options.text: Whether the student can finish the whole month in one weekend
options.feedback: The routine is about one realistic week at a time.

6-study-skills-executive-functioning-pla.explanation: Before finalizing the plan, check for conflicts, overload, missing materials, and whether the plan is realistic.
